comments.reader: Asel has achieved the age-related standard in reading. Asel is a developing reader who is building her confidence across a range of texts and has made steady progress through Maple Learning Zone this year. Her reading fluency is developing well and she is becoming more accurate and assured when reading aloud. Retrieval is also growing — Asel is increasingly able to locate and record information from texts with greater independence. The two areas that will make the biggest difference to Asel's progress in Year 5 are inference and vocabulary. Inference — drawing conclusions about characters' feelings, thoughts and motives from evidence within the text — is the skill that most separates developing from secure readers at this stage, and it is the key area for Asel to focus on. Building her reading vocabulary through wider, more regular reading will support this directly. Reading every day from a range of quality texts will be central to her continued growth.
comments.writer: Asel has achieved the age-related standard in writing. Asel has made steady progress as a writer this year and is becoming more confident in putting her ideas onto the page. Her spelling is developing well and she is applying a growing range of key year group spelling patterns with greater accuracy. Her handwriting is also developing and is becoming more consistent across her writing. The areas that will have the greatest impact on Asel's progress in Year 5 are composition and punctuation. Strengthening how she plans and sequences ideas — building a clear structure across paragraphs — will help her writing feel more purposeful and controlled. Alongside this, growing her confidence with punctuation such as inverted commas for direct speech and apostrophes will lift the accuracy of her writing significantly. Making more ambitious vocabulary choices, drawing on the texts she reads, is an important next step. With continued effort and attention to these areas, Asel has every reason to grow strongly as a writer.
comments.mathematician: Asel has achieved the age-related standard in mathematics. Asel is developing as a mathematician across the range of concepts covered in Year 4 and has a genuine strength to build on: her times table knowledge is excellent. Strong multiplication fact recall is one of the most important foundations in Year 4 mathematics and Asel's fluency here has supported her developing confidence with fractions and decimals. Where Asel will benefit from the most focus in Year 5 is in mathematical reasoning and problem solving — moving from knowing how to calculate to being able to explain her thinking and choose the right approach when a problem is unfamiliar. Written calculation is developing and, with continued practice using the column method for addition, subtraction and short multiplication, her accuracy will grow. Asel should feel proud of the strong multiplicative foundation she has built this year — it will serve her well as the demands of Year 5 mathematics increase.
comments.learner_21c: Asel, you have had a really positive year as a 21st Century Learner in Maple Learning Zone and there is a great deal to be proud of. You are a highly independent learner — you take real ownership of your learning and consistently show the drive to see things through on your own terms, which is a genuine strength. You are also a wonderful collaborator, and the way you contribute to group learning and support other learners around you makes a real difference to the whole learning zone. As a historian, your collaboration skills came through clearly during our enquiry into the legacy of the Mayan civilisation. You contributed thoughtfully to your group, sharing ideas and helping to build a fuller understanding of how this ancient civilisation continues to shape the world today. One important area of growth for you in Year 5 will be your oracy — you are developing your confidence to share ideas in home zone discussions and the more you take those moments to contribute your voice, the more your thinking will grow. Keep building your resilience too, Asel, and trust that the effort you put in each day is what makes the biggest difference. You have strong character and a lot to offer — keep going.
comments.rights: Asel, you show a strong and consistent understanding of your rights and responsibilities as a learner and this has been one of the real hallmarks of your year in Maple Learning Zone. You are respectful, considered and genuinely attentive to the learning of other learners and adults around you. The way you show up for others — whether in group learning, paired activity or across the wider learning zone — reflects real strength of character. Your learning dispositions are strong and your approach to everything you do reflects a learner who takes her responsibilities seriously. In Year 5, continue to bring these qualities with you, Asel. They are noticed, they matter and they make Maple Learning Zone a better place to learn. Keep being the learner you are.

comments.reader: Haris has achieved the age-related standard in reading. He reads with confidence and fluency, using intonation and punctuation to bring texts to life in a way that genuinely supports his understanding of what he is reading. His vocabulary is a real strength — Haris has a wide and well-developed bank of words that he draws on when discussing and responding to texts. He is developing both his retrieval and inference skills: he can locate information from a text and is growing in his ability to draw conclusions about characters' thoughts, feelings and motives from evidence on the page. Strengthening this skill — moving from what a text says to what it means — will be central to his progress as a reader in Year 5. Reading more widely every day, and actively questioning what he reads, will support Haris in building the depth of comprehension that his fluency and vocabulary already have the potential to unlock.
comments.writer: Haris has achieved the age-related standard in writing. His writing has several genuine strengths: his ideas are well-shaped and inventive, his vocabulary choices are ambitious and well-judged, and his spelling — including key year group spelling patterns — is consistently accurate. Haris is particularly confident as a non-fiction writer, where his ability to organise and present information clearly really comes through. The two areas to develop are punctuation and grammar, where accuracy with features such as inverted commas, apostrophes and subordinate clauses needs to become more consistent, and handwriting, where forming letters more precisely and joining fluently will help Haris get his strong ideas onto the page with greater ease and speed. These are the areas receiving focus in his additional support. Prioritising careful, precise handwriting and proofreading his punctuation as he edits will make a significant difference to the impact of his writing in Year 5.
comments.mathematician: Haris has achieved the age-related standard in mathematics. He is a capable mathematician with real strength across a broad range of concepts covered in Year 4 — his written calculations are accurate and confident, and he approaches fractions and decimals with a clarity that many of his peers are still building. His mathematical reasoning is strong: Haris can explain his thinking clearly and contributes well in home zone discussions. Problem solving is an area with room to grow, particularly when a question requires him to identify the most efficient approach before calculating. His times table recall is developing and, as the mathematical demands of Year 5 increase, building sharper fluency here will directly support his learning with more complex fractions and division. Regular, focused practice with his multiplication facts will be a worthwhile investment for Haris as he moves into Year 5.
comments.learner_21c:
Haris, you have had a positive year in Maple Learning Zone and there is much to be proud of. Your independence is one of your standout qualities — you are very capable of getting started on your own, sustaining your focus and making decisions about your learning without needing to be prompted. Your oracy is also a real strength: you contribute with clarity and confidence in home zone discussions and your spoken ideas are often some of the most well-developed in the room.

As a scientist, you brought real curiosity and imagination to our enquiry into how electricity flows in a circuit. You asked searching questions about what you were observing, made thoughtful predictions and showed a genuine desire to understand the principles behind the practical learning — not just to complete it.

As a geographer, you showed impressive independence when exploring the physical and human geography of South America. You took ownership of your learning, selected and organised information with confidence and drew clear connections between what you discovered and the wider world.

The area to focus on in Year 5 is resilience. When learning becomes difficult or unfamiliar, keeping going through that discomfort is what separates good learners from great ones — and you have everything you need to do that. Your curiosity and collaboration with other learners are both developing well and will become increasingly important as expectations grow. Keep pushing yourself, Haris.
comments.rights: Haris, you show a good understanding of your rights and responsibilities as a learner and bring a thoughtful, considered approach to home zone life. You listen respectfully to other learners and adults and contribute to discussions with care. Your knowledge of different beliefs, traditions and ways of life — including your own faith — enriches the home zone and helps those around you to develop a deeper understanding of the world. This is something to be genuinely proud of. As your confidence in your rights and responsibilities continues to grow, the next step is to bring that same thoughtfulness and consistency to every part of your learning day — including those moments when challenges feel hard. Keep building these qualities into Year 5, Haris.

comments.reader: Daisy has not yet achieved the age-related standard in reading and is having additional support and provision to develop her skills. Daisy is at an early stage in building her reading fluency, and developing the confidence and pace to read expressively will be an important focus going into Year 5. Her strongest skill at present is retrieval — she can locate and record information from texts with growing reliability, and this is a real positive to build from. The areas that need the most attention are inference and reading vocabulary. Inference — drawing conclusions about characters' feelings and motives from evidence within the text — is the skill that most separates developing readers from secure ones, and Daisy is working to grow this ability. Reading every day at the right level of challenge, and pausing to think about what texts mean rather than just what they say, will be central to her progress. With consistent effort, Daisy can make real gains in Year 5.

Daisy is still building her confidence with key year group spellings and spelling patterns, and accuracy with punctuation — including apostrophes and inverted commas for direct speech — will be an important focus in Year 5. Non-fiction writing is the area where Daisy has the most ground to cover, and reading a wider range of non-fiction texts will help her understand how these are structured so she can apply those patterns in her own writing.
